title: Building recessed alcove shelving into a chimney breast
subtitle: Fit adjustable shelves in the alcoves either side of a chimney breast
excerpt: How to fit shelves into the recesses either side of a chimney breast, covering how to fix timber battens to the alcove walls, cut shelves to fit a non-square alcove, and fit an adjustable shelving system with shelf pins or side strips. Covers timber, plywood, and MDF options.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics. City and Guilds Level 2 Site Carpentry. BS 5268: Structural use of timber.

glossaryTerms.definition: A length of sawn softwood (typically 50 × 25 mm or 50 × 38 mm) screwed to a wall to support a shelf edge. The shelf sits on top of the batten; the batten takes the load and transfers it into the wall. Battens can be notched into a shelf edge for a cleaner appearance or left visible for a workshop-style look.

content.text: The recesses either side of a chimney breast are the standard location for fitted shelving in a living room or bedroom. The alcoves are rarely square, the chimney breast seldom sits perfectly centred and the walls are rarely plumb, so every shelf needs to be measured and cut individually. The
method used here is reliable on any wall material and carries heavy loads without a dedicated shelving system.

Shelf boards in the chosen material, 18 mm MDF (easy to paint, good for living room furniture), 18 mm birch plywood (stronger and takes a painted or oiled finish), or 20 mm solid pine (traditional and takes paint or wax well). Enough for all shelves, including an allowance for the out-of-square cuts. 50 × 25 mm PAR softwood batten for the shelf supports, one length for the back wall and one for each side wall per shelf. 60 mm No. 8 screws for fixing battens to masonry walls (with matching wall plugs). Wood filler and 120-grit sandpaper for finishing.

Measure the width of the alcove at three heights: at skirting level, at the first shelf height, and at the top shelf height. Alcove walls are frequently out of plumb; the width can vary by 10 to 20 mm from floor to ceiling. Take the smallest measurement as the nominal width and allow 2 mm clearance on each side. Measure the depth of the alcove and decide on the shelf depth, 250 mm is comfortable for books, 300 mm for A4 files, 350 mm for larger items.

Mark the shelf heights on the back and side walls with the spirit level, running a level pencil line at each height. Fix the back batten first: hold it on the line, drill through the batten into the masonry at 300 mm intervals, insert wall plugs, and screw the batten down tightly. Check it is level before tightening the final screws. The back batten carries most of the load; it must be fixed into solid masonry, not into plaster alone.

Fix battens on the side walls at the same height, using the spirit level to confirm they are level with the back batten. Cut each side batten to the shelf depth minus the thickness of the back batten (so the shelf board runs to the wall behind the back batten). Check that all three battens at each shelf height are level with each other by placing a long spirit level across them.
Step 4, cut and fit the shelves

Cut each shelf to the measured width with the panel saw. Test-fit dry before fixing; the shelf should drop into the alcove with 2 mm clearance on each side. If the back wall is not square to the side walls, the back corners of the shelf will not sit flush against the rear wall; scribe the back edge of the shelf to the actual wall profile using a compass set to the widest gap and then plane or sand to the scribed line. Drop the shelf onto the battens; it can be left unfixed (gravity holds it) or screwed down through the top face if a neater finish is needed.

Fill any gaps between the shelf edge and the side walls with decorator's caulk. Apply a bead, smooth with a wet finger, and allow to dry before painting. Paint the shelves and battens together for a clean finish. Two coats of good-quality eggshell over a primer gives a durable surface for a living room shelf.

attrs.symptom: Shelf bows under load across a wide span
attrs.cause: Board material too thin for the span; 18 mm MDF over 900 mm unsupported will bow under heavy loads.
attrs.fix: Add a centre support batten fixed to the back wall, or replace the shelf with a thicker board (22 mm or 25 mm). For spans over 1000 mm, a solid timber shelf or a sandwich of two 18 mm MDF layers glued together is stronger.

attrs.symptom: Side batten pulled out of the wall on a plasterboard alcove wall
attrs.cause: Screws fixed into plasterboard only, not finding the stud behind.
attrs.fix: Use a stud detector to locate the vertical timber stud in the alcove side wall. Re-fix using longer screws (75 mm minimum) into the stud at the stud position. If no stud is accessible, use plasterboard cavity anchors rated for at least 4× the expected shelf load.
